job_description: Senior ML Engineer. Build and run RAG systems in production and communicate clearly with non-ML stakeholders.

turns.speaker: interviewer
turns.text: Tell me about a RAG system you've built and shipped to production. What did the architecture look like, and what were the hardest engineering challenges you faced?

turns.speaker: candidate
turns.text:
Sure. At my last company we built a RAG system for internal legal document search — about 2 million documents, averaging 40 pages each. The architecture was a two-stage retrieval pipeline: a dense retriever using a fine-tuned bi-encoder based on BGE-large, followed by a cross-encoder reranker to reorder the top-50 candidates down to top-5 before hitting GPT-4. We chunked documents at 512 tokens with a 64-token overlap and stored embeddings in Pinecone with metadata filters for jurisdiction and document type.

The hardest challenge was honestly retrieval latency at the reranking stage. The cross-encoder was adding around 800ms p95, which was unacceptable for our 1.5-second SLA. We solved that by batching the 50 candidates into a single forward pass, moving to a distilled reranker — ms-marco-MiniLM-L-6 — and deploying it on a GPU-backed endpoint. That brought reranking latency down to about 120ms p95. The other major challenge was chunking strategy: naive fixed-size chunking was splitting contract clauses mid-sentence and tanking retrieval precision. We switched to a recursive, semantics-aware splitter that respected sentence and section boundaries, which lifted our top-5 recall from around 61% to 78% on our internal eval set.

turns.speaker: interviewer
turns.text: What failure modes did you see in production that you didn't anticipate during development, and how did you handle them?

turns.speaker: candidate
turns.text:
Two big ones. First was context stuffing failures — when the top-5 retrieved chunks were all highly similar, the LLM would essentially hallucinate a synthesis rather than pull a direct answer, because it had no diversity in the context window. We caught this through a user feedback loop we'd instrumented; about 12% of 'thumbs down' ratings traced back to this. The fix was a maximal marginal relevance step post-reranking to enforce diversity among the final chunks, which dropped that failure category by roughly 60%.

The second was embedding model drift. We fine-tuned our bi-encoder on a dataset of 80k query-document pairs from our domain, but after about four months, new document types started getting added — regulatory filings we hadn't seen during training — and our recall on those dropped to around 45%. We hadn't set up any retrieval quality monitoring, so we only caught it when users started complaining. After that, we built an automated evaluation pipeline that runs weekly on a stratified holdout set and pages us if recall drops more than 5 percentage points. It's the kind of thing that feels obvious in retrospect, but you need the production signal to prioritize it.

turns.speaker: interviewer
turns.text: Imagine you need to present the state of this system to a VP of Legal who has no ML background. She wants to know if she can trust it. How do you approach that conversation?

turns.speaker: candidate
turns.text:
I'd start by reframing the question from 'can you trust it' to 'here's exactly when you can trust it and when you shouldn't' — because a blanket yes or no would be dishonest and ultimately less useful to her. I'd avoid terms like recall and precision entirely and instead anchor on things she already cares about. Something like: 'In our testing on 500 questions your team actually asked, the system surfaced the right document in the top three results 78% of the time. For the other 22%, it either retrieved something partially relevant or missed entirely — and those cases cluster around very recent filings from the last 90 days, because the index updates nightly.'

I'd also be explicit about the risk profile. Legal is a high-stakes domain, so I'd tell her directly: 'This is a research acceleration tool, not a replacement for attorney review. We'd recommend treating any answer it gives as a starting point that still needs a human check before it's acted on.' I find that being upfront about limitations actually builds more trust than overselling. And I'd close by showing her the monitoring dashboard we built — not to impress her with graphs, but to show her that we know when it's degrading and we have a process to fix it. That tends to land well with non-technical stakeholders: they want to know there's a human in the loop watching the system, not just the system watching itself.

job_description: Senior ML Engineer. Build and run RAG systems in production and communicate clearly with non-ML stakeholders.

turns.speaker: interviewer
turns.text: Can you walk me through a RAG system you've built and run in production? What were the key design decisions you made?

turns.speaker: candidate
turns.text: Sure. At my last company we built a RAG system for internal legal document search — about 2 million documents, averaging 8 pages each. The first big decision was chunking strategy. We tried fixed 512-token chunks but saw a 23% drop in answer relevance scores on our eval set compared to semantic chunking with a sliding overlap. We landed on 400-token chunks with a 10% overlap, which gave us a retrieval precision@5 of around 0.81. The second decision was the retriever itself. We ran a hybrid setup — BM25 for lexical recall and a fine-tuned bi-encoder for dense retrieval — then fused the scores using reciprocal rank fusion. That closed a gap we had on short, keyword-heavy queries where the dense retriever alone was struggling, bringing recall@10 up from 0.74 to 0.89. On the generation side, we used GPT-4 with a context window budget of 3,000 tokens for retrieved chunks, and we monitored hallucination rate using a separate NLI-based faithfulness scorer. We held that below 4% in production.

turns.speaker: interviewer
turns.text: What were the biggest failure modes you encountered once it was live, and how did you handle them?

turns.speaker: candidate
turns.text: Two stand out. The first was retrieval drift — over time, new documents were added with slightly different terminology than the original corpus, and our dense embeddings hadn't seen that vocabulary. We noticed it because our user satisfaction scores dropped about 12 points over three months while our automated eval metrics stayed flat. The automated evals were testing against a fixed benchmark that didn't reflect the new document styles. We fixed it by adding a monthly embedding model refresh on the new document slice and expanding our eval set dynamically using user thumbs-down feedback. The second failure mode was context stuffing — when multiple retrieved chunks were highly redundant, the model would essentially just repeat the same point from slightly different angles and miss covering other relevant aspects of the answer. We diagnosed this by logging chunk-level cosine similarities in the retrieved set and found that in about 18% of queries, three or more chunks had pairwise similarity above 0.93. We added a maximal marginal relevance re-ranking step, which reduced that overlap rate to under 5% and improved our answer completeness scores by about 15% on a human eval rubric.

turns.speaker: interviewer
turns.text: This system clearly has a lot of technical nuance. How do you explain the performance and reliability of a system like this to, say, a Head of Legal who owns the product but has no ML background?

turns.speaker: candidate
turns.text: I try to anchor everything to outcomes they already care about. So instead of talking about retrieval precision or faithfulness scores, I'd say something like: 'Right now, when someone asks the system a question, it finds the right source documents about 89% of the time — think of it like a paralegal who pulls the correct case files almost every time. When it misses, it's usually on very niche or recently filed documents, and here's what we're doing to close that gap.' For the hallucination issue, I'd frame it as: 'Less than 4 out of every 100 answers contain a claim that isn't directly supported by the documents — and we have an automated checker running continuously to catch those.' I also make sure to be upfront about tradeoffs. When we added the monthly model refresh, I told the Head of Legal that it would reduce the drift problem but introduce a small window — about two to three days — where newly ingested documents might not be as well-indexed. She actually appreciated that honesty because it helped her set the right expectations with her team rather than being surprised. I find that stakeholders trust the system more when you're transparent about its limits, not just its wins.

job_description: Senior ML Engineer. Build and run RAG systems in production and communicate clearly with non-ML stakeholders.

turns.speaker: interviewer
turns.text: Let's start with RAG systems. Can you walk me through how you've architected a RAG pipeline in production, and what the key design decisions were?

turns.speaker: candidate
turns.text: Sure. The most recent one I built was for an internal knowledge base at a fintech company — about 2 million documents, mix of PDFs and structured policy text. The core decisions came down to three things: chunking strategy, retrieval architecture, and reranking. For chunking, we tried fixed 512-token windows first, but that hurt precision badly on long regulatory documents — we were getting chunks that split mid-clause. We moved to a semantic chunking approach using sentence embeddings to find natural breakpoints, which brought our chunk-level relevance score up from about 61% to 78% on our eval set. For retrieval, we ran a hybrid setup — dense retrieval with a fine-tuned BGE-Large model alongside BM25 — and fused the scores with Reciprocal Rank Fusion. That alone improved end-to-end answer correctness from 71% to 83% on our human-labeled golden set. The reranker was a cross-encoder — Cohere's at the time — sitting in front of the LLM call. The main tradeoff there was latency: adding the reranker pushed p95 latency from 1.1 seconds to 2.4 seconds, which we had to justify to the product team.

turns.speaker: interviewer
turns.text: You mentioned latency as a tradeoff. How did you handle that conversation with the product team, and what was the outcome?

turns.speaker: candidate
turns.text: Yeah, that was a real negotiation. The product team's instinct was 'just cut the reranker,' and honestly I understood where they were coming from — 2.4 seconds felt sluggish in a chat-style UI. So instead of defending the technical choice in the abstract, I framed it around a metric they already cared about: support ticket deflection rate. I showed them a two-week A/B test where the reranker variant deflected 34% of inbound tickets versus 21% for the baseline. I put a rough dollar figure on it — about $40K per month in avoided agent costs — and compared that to a latency improvement that, per our own user research, users didn't actually notice below 3 seconds. They approved keeping it. We also partially mitigated the latency by caching embeddings for the top 5% of frequently queried documents, which brought p95 back down to 1.8 seconds.

turns.speaker: interviewer
turns.text: Great. What are the failure modes you watch most closely in a production RAG system, and how do you monitor for them?

turns.speaker: candidate
turns.text: There are three I treat as first-class concerns. The first is retrieval drift — over time, the document corpus changes but the index doesn't keep up, so you start retrieving stale or contradictory chunks. We caught this by tracking a 'corpus freshness' metric: the median age of retrieved chunks per query. When that spiked, it usually meant an ingestion pipeline had silently failed. Second is hallucination under low retrieval confidence — when the retriever doesn't find anything relevant, the LLM tends to confabulate rather than say it doesn't know. We added a retrieval confidence gate: if the top-1 similarity score fell below 0.72, we'd return a 'I don't have enough information' response rather than passing the context to the LLM. That reduced hallucination-flagged responses by about 60% in our human review sample. Third is context window misuse — stuffing too many chunks in and having the model fixate on the first and last ones due to the lost-in-the-middle effect. We experimented with chunk ordering strategies and settled on placing the highest-scored chunk first and second-highest last, which improved answer grounding noticeably. We monitor all of this with a combination of LLM-as-judge scoring on a daily sample and human review on flagged edge cases.

There's also stuff like hybrid search where you combine semantic search with keyword search. It's really about iterating and testing different approaches to see what works best for your data.

I might use an analogy, like it's similar to a person skimming a document quickly — they might miss things sometimes.

turns.speaker: interviewer

Basically you want to catch issues before users start complaining. I've worked with some of these tools before, though the exact setup tends to vary a lot by company and use case.

job_description: Data Scientist. Design and analyze experiments (A/B tests) and explain results and tradeoffs to product teams.

turns.speaker: interviewer
turns.text: Let's start with experiment design. Walk me through how you would set up an A/B test to evaluate a new onboarding flow for a mobile app. What are the key decisions you'd need to make before launching?

turns.speaker: candidate
turns.text: Sure. The first thing I'd nail down is the primary metric — in this case I'd probably choose something like 7-day retention or completion rate of the onboarding flow itself, because day-1 activation can be gamed by a flashy design that doesn't actually drive long-term value. Then I'd do a power analysis: assuming a baseline 7-day retention of, say, 35%, if we want to detect a 3 percentage point lift with 80% power at a 5% significance level, you're looking at roughly 7,000 users per variant. I'd also pre-register a handful of guardrail metrics — things like session length on day 1, support ticket volume, and crash rate — so we don't accidentally optimize retention by overwhelming users with tooltips they can't dismiss. On the randomization side, I'd randomize at the user level rather than the session level to avoid contamination, and I'd make sure to exclude users who were mid-onboarding at launch so we don't get a polluted control group.

turns.speaker: interviewer
turns.text: Good. Now suppose the test runs and you see a statistically significant 4-point lift in 7-day retention, but your guardrail metric — day-1 session length — dropped by about 12%. How do you present that to the product team and what do you recommend?

turns.speaker: candidate
turns.text: I'd lead with the tension rather than burying it. Something like: 'We have a real signal on retention, but we also have a real signal that users are spending less time in the app on day one, and we need to understand which story is true before we ship.' Concretely, I'd dig into whether the session length drop is mechanistic — maybe the new flow is just faster and more efficient, so users complete onboarding in 4 minutes instead of 7, and then they churn less because they weren't frustrated. If that's the case, shorter session length is actually a feature, not a bug. But if I slice the data and see that the session length drop is concentrated among users who never made it past step 3 of onboarding, that's a drop-off problem that'll hurt us at 30-day retention even if 7-day looks fine. I'd present both hypotheses with the supporting cuts, give a clear recommendation — probably a hold with a targeted fix to step 3 — and set a decision deadline so the team isn't stuck in analysis paralysis. I'd also flag that we should extend the observation window to 30 days before a full rollout, because a 4-point lift that reverses at day 30 is worse than no lift at all.

turns.speaker: interviewer
turns.text: That's a great point about longer-term effects. More broadly, what are the biggest failure modes you've seen or worried about in A/B testing programs, and how do you guard against them?

turns.speaker: candidate
turns.text: A few come to mind. The biggest one in practice is peeking — teams check results daily and call the test the moment p hits 0.05, which inflates false positive rates dramatically. In a simulation I ran at my last job, peeking daily on a 2-week test pushed the effective false positive rate from 5% to nearly 26%. We fixed that by adopting sequential testing with an alpha-spending function, which let us look at the data continuously without blowing up the error rate. The second failure mode is novelty effect — users engage more with anything new, so a 2-week test on a UI change can look like a win that completely evaporates by week 6. We started requiring a 'novelty decay check' for any surface-level UI experiment, basically holding a 10% holdout for an additional 4 weeks after the main test concluded. Third is network effects and interference — if you're testing a social or collaborative feature and you randomize at the user level, treatment users interact with control users and you get spillover that contaminates both groups. For those cases I'd push for cluster randomization, even though it costs you statistical power and you need a lot more clusters to compensate. And finally, there's metric misalignment — optimizing a proxy metric that diverges from the business outcome. I always try to get sign-off from product and leadership on the primary metric before the test launches, not after, so there's no goalpost-moving when results are inconvenient.

job_description: Data Scientist. Design and analyze experiments (A/B tests) and explain results and tradeoffs to product teams.

turns.speaker: interviewer
turns.text: Let's start with experiment design. Walk me through how you would design an A/B test to evaluate a new recommendation algorithm on an e-commerce platform.

turns.speaker: candidate
turns.text: Sure. First, I'd nail down the primary metric — in this case probably revenue per session, since click-through rate alone can be gamed by flashy but low-converting recommendations. I'd also pre-register a few guardrail metrics: cart abandonment rate, page load time, and customer support contacts, so we catch regressions even if the primary metric looks good. For sample size, I'd run a power calculation targeting 80% power at a 5% significance level to detect a minimum detectable effect of, say, 2% lift in revenue per session — based on historical variance that usually means roughly 50,000 sessions per arm, which at our traffic levels is about a two-week runtime. I'd randomize at the user level rather than the session level to avoid carryover effects where the same user sees both variants. One failure mode I'd flag upfront is novelty effect — users might click more on anything new — so I'd plan to look at the last week of data separately to see if the lift holds or decays.

turns.speaker: interviewer
turns.text: Good. Now suppose the test results come back and the new algorithm shows a statistically significant 3% lift in revenue per session, but a 5% increase in cart abandonment. How do you communicate that tradeoff to the product team?

turns.speaker: candidate
turns.text: I'd lead with the business impact in dollar terms, not p-values. So I might say: 'The new algorithm is projected to add roughly $1.2M in annualized revenue, but we're also seeing about 800 more abandoned carts per day, which based on our historical recovery rate of 15% from email flows represents roughly $300K in annualized lost revenue — so the net expected gain is closer to $900K.' Then I'd be explicit about what we don't know: is the abandonment caused by the recommendations surfacing higher-priced items that users aren't ready to buy, or is it a UX friction issue? I'd recommend we ship to 10% of traffic, instrument funnel drop-off by price tier, and re-evaluate in three weeks before a full rollout. I find product teams respond much better when you give them a clear decision framework — here's what we'd gain, here's the risk, here's the next piece of evidence we need — rather than just handing them a p-value and a chart.

turns.speaker: interviewer
turns.text: What's a situation where you pushed back on shipping a winning experiment, and how did you handle it?

turns.speaker: candidate
turns.text: Yeah, this came up about a year ago. We ran a test on a new checkout flow that showed a statistically significant 4% lift in conversion rate, and the product team was ready to ship immediately. But when I dug into the segment breakdown, the lift was entirely driven by mobile users on iOS — desktop and Android were flat or slightly negative. The overall significance was real, but it was masking a heterogeneous treatment effect. I brought that analysis to the PM and the engineering lead with a concrete recommendation: don't do a full rollout yet; instead, ship only to iOS mobile, which was about 40% of our traffic, and run a follow-up test on the other segments with a redesigned variant. I also flagged that if we shipped universally, we'd be degrading experience for 60% of users to get a blended average lift, which felt like the wrong call. The PM was initially frustrated because they'd been waiting on this feature, but once I showed the segment revenue breakdown on a single slide — iOS gain vs. desktop loss in dollar terms — they agreed to the phased approach. The iOS-only ship ended up being a clean win, and the follow-up test for desktop found a different layout that worked better there too.

job_description: Data Scientist. Design and analyze experiments (A/B tests) and explain results and tradeoffs to product teams.

turns.speaker: interviewer
turns.text: Let's start with experiment design. Walk me through how you would set up an A/B test to evaluate a new onboarding flow for a mobile app. What are the key decisions you'd make upfront?

turns.speaker: candidate
turns.text: Sure. The first thing I'd nail down is the primary metric — in this case I'd probably push for something like Day-7 retention or a meaningful activation event, like 'user completes their first core action,' rather than a vanity metric like sign-up rate. Then I'd do a power calculation: assuming we want to detect a 3 percentage-point lift on a 25% baseline retention rate, with 80% power and a 5% significance level, we'd need roughly 4,500 users per arm. I'd also decide on the randomization unit upfront — user-level here, not session-level, to avoid contamination. And I'd set the experiment duration based on both the power calculation and business cycles, so at least two full weeks to smooth out day-of-week effects. Finally, I'd pre-register the primary metric and any guardrail metrics — things like support ticket volume or crash rate — so we're not fishing for significance after the fact.

turns.speaker: interviewer
turns.text: Good. Now suppose the test runs and you see a statistically significant 4-point lift in Day-7 retention, but your checkout conversion rate — a guardrail metric — dropped by 1.5%. How do you present that to the product team?

turns.speaker: candidate
turns.text: I'd lead with the tension, not bury it. I'd say something like: 'The new onboarding flow is working — we're retaining more users in the first week — but we're seeing a signal that those users are converting to paid at a lower rate, and we need to understand why before we ship.' Then I'd quantify the business impact of both sides. If we have, say, 50,000 new users a month, a 4-point retention lift is 2,000 more active users, but a 1.5-point checkout drop on a 20% baseline is a 7.5% relative decline in conversions — that could easily outweigh the retention gain in revenue terms depending on LTV. I'd walk through a few hypotheses: maybe the new flow attracts a different user segment, maybe it sets wrong expectations about the product, or maybe it's a fluke in the data given the guardrail wasn't our primary metric. I'd recommend either a follow-up deep-dive segmentation — are the retained users lower-intent? — or a holdout period before full rollout, rather than shipping immediately. I'd also be explicit: 'This is a judgment call that involves your revenue targets, and I want to make sure we're aligned on what risk we're comfortable taking.'

turns.speaker: interviewer
turns.text: That's a solid framing. What's a failure mode in A/B testing that you've seen catch teams off guard, and how do you guard against it?

turns.speaker: candidate
turns.text: The one I've seen bite teams most often is peeking — running sequential tests without correcting for it. Teams feel pressure to ship, so they check results daily and stop as soon as p < 0.05. In one case I worked on, a team called a winner after 5 days on what turned out to be a false positive; when we re-ran the analysis at the pre-specified sample size, the effect had shrunk to noise and the confidence interval crossed zero. The fix I put in place was two-fold: first, we adopted a sequential testing framework using alpha spending — specifically an O'Brien-Fleming boundary — so teams could peek with statistical integrity. Second, I built a dashboard that showed the current p-value alongside a clear label saying 'Experiment not yet at target sample size — early results are unreliable' to make the risk visceral rather than buried in a methodology doc. It's a process and tooling problem as much as a statistics problem, and you have to solve both.

job_description: Backend Engineer. Design and operate reliable APIs and debug production incidents under pressure.

turns.speaker: interviewer
turns.text: Let's start with API design. Walk me through how you'd design a rate-limiting system for a public REST API that serves both free-tier and paid-tier customers.

turns.speaker: candidate
turns.text: Sure. First, I'd separate the rate limit policies by tier at the gateway level — something like 100 requests per minute for free and 2,000 for paid — and enforce them with a token bucket algorithm rather than a fixed window, because fixed windows have a well-known burst problem at boundary edges where a client can effectively double their allowed rate. I'd store token state in Redis using a Lua script for atomic read-modify-write, which keeps the check-and-decrement under a single round trip and avoids race conditions. The keys would be scoped to customer ID plus endpoint family, not just a global key per customer, so a client hammering one expensive endpoint doesn't eat quota from lighter-weight calls. I'd also expose rate-limit headers — X-RateLimit-Remaining, X-RateLimit-Reset — on every response so clients can back off gracefully instead of hitting 429s blind. One tradeoff I thought hard about: Redis as a single point of failure. We mitigated that by running Redis Sentinel with a failover target of under 30 seconds, and we added a local in-memory fallback that enforces a conservative limit — about 20% of the normal cap — so we degrade safely rather than opening the floodgates if Redis goes down.

turns.speaker: interviewer
turns.text: That's a solid design. Now tell me about a real production incident you debugged under pressure — walk me through your process from alert to resolution.

turns.speaker: candidate
turns.text: Yeah, one that stands out: we had a checkout API that started returning 504s at about 2 AM. P99 latency spiked from our normal 180ms to over 8 seconds within about four minutes, and our error rate crossed 12% before on-call was fully paged. I started by pulling the service's latency histogram in Datadog — I could see the spike was isolated to one specific downstream call, our inventory reservation service. I checked the inventory service's own metrics and it looked healthy — low CPU, normal throughput — so the problem wasn't inside it. That made me suspect the network path or connection pool. I looked at our HTTP client metrics and found the connection pool to inventory was exhausted: we had a max of 50 connections configured and all 50 were stuck waiting. I traced a few requests with our distributed tracing and found they were all blocking on a single database query in inventory that had ballooned from 5ms to 45 seconds. The culprit was a missing index on a column we'd added in a migration two weeks earlier — it had been fine at low volume, but a marketing campaign that night pushed inventory lookup traffic up about 6x. Fix was two-pronged: immediate — we added a query hint to force a full scan with a cap, which brought P99 back to 210ms within seven minutes. Then the proper fix the next morning was adding the composite index, which brought that query back to 3ms. Post-mortem action item was adding slow-query alerting with a 500ms threshold so we catch these before they cascade.

turns.speaker: interviewer
turns.text: You mentioned the connection pool exhaustion as a key signal. How do you think about sizing connection pools in general, and what failure modes do you watch for?

turns.speaker: candidate
turns.text: Connection pool sizing is honestly one of those things people set once and forget, which is dangerous. My starting point is Little's Law: pool size should roughly equal your average concurrency, which is throughput multiplied by average latency. So if a service handles 500 RPS to a downstream and average latency is 100ms, you need about 50 connections at steady state — and then I add maybe 20% headroom for burst. The failure modes I watch for are: pool exhaustion like we just saw, which you catch by tracking the 'waiting for connection' queue depth; connection leaks, where something grabs a connection and never releases it — you catch those by tracking active vs. idle connections over time and alerting when idle drops to zero for more than a minute; and then the subtler one, stale connections. If a connection sits idle long enough, the database or load balancer will close it server-side, but the client pool doesn't know, so the next request gets a broken connection. You fix that with a keep-alive ping or a max-idle-time setting shorter than the server's timeout. We set ours to 4 minutes against a 5-minute server-side timeout, which gave us a safe margin. I also think about the failure mode on the other side — too large a pool can actually hurt you by overwhelming the database with concurrent queries, so it's genuinely a two-sided optimization.

job_description: Backend Engineer. Design and operate reliable APIs and debug production incidents under pressure.

turns.speaker: interviewer
turns.text: Let's start with API design. Can you walk me through how you'd design a rate-limiting system for a public REST API? What decisions would you make and why?

turns.speaker: candidate
turns.text: Sure. The first decision is the algorithm — I'd lean toward a token bucket over a fixed window counter, because fixed windows have a burst problem at the boundary: a client can fire double the allowed requests by hitting the tail of one window and the head of the next. Token bucket smooths that out. For storage, I'd use Redis with a Lua script to make the check-and-decrement atomic — that avoids race conditions without needing a distributed lock. I'd key by API key plus endpoint, not just API key globally, so a chatty endpoint doesn't starve the others. On limits, I'd expose the headers X-RateLimit-Limit, X-RateLimit-Remaining, and X-RateLimit-Reset so clients can back off gracefully instead of hammering us. The tradeoff I'd flag is that Redis becomes a single point of failure here — so I'd run it in cluster mode with a replica, and if Redis is totally unreachable I'd fail open with a warning log rather than dropping all traffic, because a rate limiter outage shouldn't take down the whole API. That's a conscious product decision, not just a technical one.

turns.speaker: interviewer
turns.text: Good. Now let's talk about production debugging. Tell me about a time you had to diagnose a serious incident — what was the problem and how did you work through it?

turns.speaker: candidate
turns.text: Yeah, we had a bad one about a year ago. Our p99 latency on the order-creation endpoint spiked from around 120ms to over 4 seconds, and our error rate on that endpoint hit about 18% — all 503s. This was a Friday evening, peak traffic. First thing I did was check our dashboards — CPU and memory on the API pods were fine, so it wasn't a resource saturation issue. I then looked at downstream dependencies and saw our PostgreSQL connection pool was exhausted — we were running PgBouncer in transaction mode with a pool size of 20, and we had 80 API pods each trying to hold connections during a slow DB query. I pulled slow query logs and found a query doing a sequential scan on the orders table — roughly 12 million rows — because a deploy earlier that day had introduced a filter on a non-indexed column. The fix was two-pronged: immediate — I added the index online using CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY so we didn't lock the table, and that brought p99 back to 130ms within about three minutes. Longer term, we added query plan analysis to our CI pipeline using pgMustard so that class of regression gets caught before it ships. We also tuned PgBouncer pool sizing relative to pod count, which we'd honestly just neglected.

turns.speaker: interviewer
turns.text: When you say you failed open on the rate limiter if Redis is unreachable — how do you make sure that decision doesn't get abused? And how would you know it's even happening?

turns.speaker: candidate
turns.text: Great question — failing open without observability is basically the same as having no rate limiter. So I'd instrument it explicitly: any request that bypasses the limit check because Redis was unavailable gets tagged with a bypass_reason=redis_unavailable label on the metric. I'd set an alert if that count exceeds, say, 1% of total requests over a 5-minute window — at that point it's not a blip, it's a real Redis problem and on-call needs to know. On the abuse side, the honest answer is that a prolonged Redis outage does create a window where a bad actor could hammer us. To bound that risk, I'd make sure our API pods themselves have a lightweight in-memory fallback — a simple fixed-window counter per pod — so we're not completely blind. It won't be perfectly coordinated across pods, but it limits the blast radius to maybe 10x normal traffic per pod rather than unlimited. And I'd document the failure mode explicitly in the runbook so it's not an implicit assumption that only I know about.

job_description: Backend Engineer. Design and operate reliable APIs and debug production incidents under pressure.

turns.speaker: interviewer
turns.text: Let's start with API design. Walk me through how you'd design a rate-limiting system for a public REST API that serves both free-tier and paid-tier customers.

turns.speaker: candidate
turns.text:
Sure. The first decision is where to enforce rate limits — I'd put it at the API gateway layer so it's enforced before requests hit any application servers, which keeps the cost of a rejected request very low. For the storage backend I'd use Redis with a sliding window counter rather than a fixed window, because fixed windows have a well-known burst problem: a client can double their effective rate by hitting the boundary between two windows. Sliding window costs a bit more in Redis ops — roughly two commands instead of one — but it's worth it for fairness.

For limits themselves, I'd tier them: free users get, say, 100 requests per minute per API key, paid users get 2,000. I'd return standard headers — X-RateLimit-Limit, X-RateLimit-Remaining, and Retry-After on a 429 — so clients can back off gracefully. One tradeoff I'd flag: Redis is a single point of failure here. I'd mitigate that with a replica and a circuit breaker that fails open — meaning if Redis is unreachable, we allow traffic through rather than taking the whole API down. That's a deliberate product decision, and I'd document it explicitly.

turns.speaker: interviewer
turns.text: Good. Now let's shift to production debugging. Tell me about a real incident where an API you owned degraded in production. How did you find the root cause?

turns.speaker: candidate
turns.text:
Yeah, we had one about eight months ago. Our order-submission API's p99 latency spiked from around 120ms to over 4 seconds, and our error rate on 5xx responses went from under 0.1% to about 6% over roughly 15 minutes. Revenue impact was real — we estimated around $40k in failed checkouts during the window.

I started by ruling out the obvious: traffic volume was actually flat, so it wasn't a load spike. I pulled our distributed traces in Jaeger and immediately saw that the database call inside the order write path was ballooning — the span for a Postgres INSERT was taking 3 to 3.5 seconds when it normally takes under 10ms. I checked pg_stat_activity and found hundreds of transactions in a 'lock wait' state, all blocked behind a single long-running transaction. Turned out a data migration script a teammate had kicked off manually was holding an ACCESS EXCLUSIVE lock on the orders table — it needed to add a column. The migration had no timeout set, so it just sat there blocking everything.

Fix was immediate: kill the migration transaction, latency recovered within 30 seconds. Then the proper fix: we added a lock_timeout of 200ms and statement_timeout to all migration scripts, and we mandated that any schema change on a high-traffic table go through an online migration pattern — using tools like pgroll or doing the expand-contract pattern — so we never take a full table lock in production again.

turns.speaker: interviewer
turns.text: You mentioned failing open on the Redis circuit breaker. How do you think about the risks of that decision, and are there cases where you'd fail closed instead?

turns.speaker: candidate
turns.text:
Failing open is the right default for a rate limiter on most APIs because the blast radius of Redis being down is probably a few minutes of unmetered traffic — annoying commercially, but recoverable. The blast radius of failing closed is taking your entire API offline, which is almost always worse. So you're trading a bounded abuse risk for availability.

That said, there are clear cases where I'd flip to fail closed. If the API is serving something with hard safety or compliance constraints — think financial transactions with strict per-user limits for regulatory reasons, or an API where abuse has direct fraud implications — then you can't afford to fail open. In those cases I'd also invest more heavily in the Redis availability story: Redis Cluster across multiple AZs, maybe even a local in-memory fallback with a very short TTL so you have at least approximate limiting even if the central store is degraded. The key thing is making that tradeoff explicitly and getting sign-off from product and legal, not just leaving it as an undocumented engineering decision.
